author: @mac-qt3wd
text: Hi Rob, I discovered much like a light bulb a speaker (for a PC) can not tell the difference between ac and dc voltage and it also uses less power with dc. The wires became thin and brittle at the plug and eventually snapped, so I hooked it up to a motorcycle battery, doesn't matter which way round the wires go either which I found odd. Used about 0.1 amps at 12 volts, the speakers when plugged into mains were 55 watts consumption.

replies.author: @mac-qt3wd
replies.text: Actually just checked again and the speakers were 18 watts not 55 my bad, dunno where I got 55 From!! Must have been looking at something else, still 18 watts on ac and 12 x 0.1 = 1.2 watts on dc. That's a massive saving percentage wise

author: @mouse_mc
text: That is awesome. Are you still interested in the earth battery idea btw? I think the name needs to change. Did some tests myself and found that while the chosen material matter, the size and shape does not seem to matter. Or the amount of soil and so in. Saw a clever dude who made a tiny bank of these batteries into about a AA size. What I found really interesting was when testing continuity across various of these experiments, the fact that the buzzer went off amazed me. Sometimes I wouldn't get continuity buzzer, but do get volts. My last test was PVC conduit with tiny holes at varying distances, filled with plain water and sealed the ends, I got a voltage of about 0.5 and distances did not make a difference. My conclusion is that smaller is better. This really sound like a 3d print idea. Like the ice tray, but much smaller and tiny tiny holes the diameter of your copper and steel wires so they can be filled and wires inserted easily. We know electrons are not really moving through wires and that is actually the fields around the wires and the wires only direct them. This is why I find it fascinating and all the stuff you do lol

replies.author: @mouse_mc
replies.text: Oh and most important, water seems to be the key ingredient. So does water have a special field we are not aware of? We know water is a unique liquid, non like it. Take also conductors, we think we need them alone, like adding salt to make water conductive, but static doesn't require a conductor. Sorry, my head going crazy again

replies.author: @marcfruchtman9473
replies.text: @mouse_mc water mixes with the soil to create ions (charged atoms and molecules) which allows the ions in the soil to conduct. As I have mentioned before (to others) is this system is just creating a "battery"... where moist soil acts as the electrolyte. It is basically a very poor battery. Most of the energy comes from the zinc and copper reaction and very very little from anywhere else. You can prove this to yourself by testing for "milliamps of current" with a multimeter to see how much current you get with a dead short... and then remove the zinc and replace it with just copper... now there can't be any zinc copper action and you will find the current of "just the soil interacting with copper".

replies.author: @mouse_mc
replies.text: @marcfruchtman9473 I don't have zinc unfortunately and the amps are too low for my multimeter to detect anything. But I do think your wrong with regards to the reaction happening. Because I still got results by testing just plain water. And the chosen metals only have to be different. It is was a chemical reaction like a battery then bigger sizes would matter, but it doesn't. But it is because we don't really know what is happening that I like exploring this idea. Anycase there is a lot of testing to still do. Plus I live in South Africa and we don't have power about half the day, so that frees me up to just test things like this, without any preconceptions cheers

replies.author: @marcfruchtman9473
replies.text: @mouse_mc Sigh. "Too low to detect" literally says it all. Please, if you don't have power half the day...there are far better ways to generate power than "Too low to detect" What resources are around you?.

replies.author: @mouse_mc
replies.text: @marcfruchtman9473 I never said I'm looking into this to generate power for myself, there are much easier ways. What I am saying is it is interesting and I have time to mess around because I have plenty time without power which means no interference from grid power. And yes, I just have the cheapest multimeter. Sigh, where is your sense of adventure or do you just know it all and say there is no need to experiment. Sigh sigh

author: @Route72Trucks
text:
My last post was giving me trouble. I’m going to try it again.

Your ring magnets are curious. I have often considered trying to shield one of its poles with several layers of magnetic shielding material. Wondering if this would allow you to greatly reduce your chosen magnetic pole.

Creating an imbalance allowing the magnet to enter one side easier than it would have otherwise. Possibly propelling a magnet passing through the center faster out one side than the force required to get into the center of the magnet.

replies.author: @marcfruchtman9473
replies.text: Not exactly sure what you mean by "faster out one side required to get into the center of the magnet" because it seems to imply more energy out than in. (Unless you are saying something else?) But otherwise there is not a way to do that. (That violates the principle of Conservation of Energy.) However, you might be able to arrange it where it takes much more force over time to push something inward, and then comes out faster but with less force over time.

replies.author: @Route72Trucks
replies.text:
@marcfruchtman9473   Thank you for your response and yes you understood my ramblings fairly well.  

I understand what the fundamental laws are.  I am not proposing a change to those understandings, simply a cleverly designed shield that redirects the chosen magnetic force.  

Magnet switches do this.  Allowing the user to switch the force of the magnet essentially on or off.   Of course it’s not turning the magnet off, it’s just positioning a magnetic shield in a fashion that the force is nearly canceled out, or moving the flux away from the chosen area.  
 
The same concept just in a way that would allow you to (in few words) create a magnet that who’s “repulsion” phase is acting on the center more strongly on the outgoing side of the ring.

replies.author: @Route72Trucks
replies.text:
Let’s say we are passing one small magnet through the center of the larger ring.  The small magnet is traveling  in a straight line through the opening in Robs ring magnet and out the other side.   The magnetic faild of Rob’s ring magnet should more or less look like a donut.  When the small Magnet travels in a straight line toward the opening of the ring magnet it will be encountering either a push, or pull force that is equal to its counterpart should the small magnet be traveling through the opening and out the other side.  Canceling any possible net gain.   Laws of physics right.  

What if we designed a shield to redirect the flux on the incoming side of the magnet in a way to allow the small traveling magnet to encounter less direct force coming into the ring than the push it will revive from the non shrilled side of the magnet.

replies.author: @marcfruchtman9473
replies.text:
@Route72Trucks Ok... yes, that makes it more clear. So, one of the most interesting things about magnetic field lines is that they follow a circuit path. Now, I have to say this never made any sense to me until recently. Basically, all of "space" (and air) is mostly "empty" and therefore it has a very similar "conductivity" to a magnetic field. You can generally think of this term as the magnetic Permeability of "Free Space" and the magnetic permeability of air is essentially equal to the vacuum of space. Rather than use the actual number they call it "relative permeabilityand call that =~1) Now imagine I said... that it is all like a "wire" conducting magnetic field lines...  So, now you sort of get how magnetic lines of force are "conducted". However, "soft iron" is much much more conductive (permeable) to magnetic fields... essentially, (not the same as a shield) but essentially, if you place a piece of soft iron (called a keeper) onto the ends of a horse shoe shaped magnet  almost all of the magnetic lines of force are "conducted" into that iron piece, and almost NO lines of force will go beyond that. Ie, it concentrates the magnetic circuit into the iron. Thus, you can sort of create your idea, by molding soft iron over the areas where you want "less" force because the iron will pull the magnetic lines of force into it. (If that makes some sense?) However, I do believe that you have to make the "circuit" for it to be most effective.
A decent video on this topic is: Magnetic Fields, Keepers & Iron Filings with Horseshoe, Ceramic and Neodymium Magnets by RimstarOrg

replies.author: @Route72Trucks
replies.text: @marcfruchtman9473  thank you for the information Marc.  More things I will keep in consideration as I mentally chew on this latest of thought projects.

author: @bartronicsecurity
text: The shafts of the alternator is magnetic and if you just slide a magnet over this shaft  it will weaken the magnetic field the same way a piece of steel will weaken the open ends of a horseshoe magnet.  It will be the path least resistance path for the magnet field to flow through. So to do that conversion to the alternator correctly the shaft has to be exchanged for a non magnetic shaft such as stainless steel. That way the full power of the speaker magnet wi go into the 2 parts of the amateur core.

This reminds me of another application of bending a magnetic field.    In the US a company called MagSwitch sells magnetic clamps.    Rotate the switch to clamp to a steel surface, rotate the other way to release.   Very useful.    I have a couple of table saw feather jigs with these switches.
Dave.

author: @kreynolds1123
text:
I remember seeing one such car  alternator armature modified with a ring magnetic like Robert proposed here. I don't remember who did it, nor have I been able to find it again.

The electricity generation will be much more efficent without using slip rings to pass current through the armature coil. And the armature will be very much cooler as anresult. And while the new alternator after rectification needs to charge the batteries while the engine is idling, the field coils will generate a much higher AC voltage at higher engine speeds so the voltage needs regulating. Additionally, at a higher rpm, consuming the same power at a lower voltage reduces the amount of i² power lost in the field coils which may further increase the efficency.


As for regulating the output voltage, I propose that after rectification, there's a capacitor to help feed a smoothed voltage into a buck converter that regulates the output voltage to whats nessesary to charge a car's battery.

author: @johnzamer3142
text:
Love you sir.
I'm a beginner, anybody who can answer some questions would be appreciated. 

First was there a magnet on top of that coil? Or was it just a coil?

Second, couldn't this be applied on a bicycle or something to generate power? Or is it generating tiny amounts of power?
How does the steel bend the magnetic field? Is? It because it is a non magnetic metal.

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text:
In this video there was one small coil  on the table.  Robert  with his rotor in hand spun it over over the coil, And the alternating magnetic poles passing over the coil inducing a changing voltage in the coil. 

Can this be done in a bicycle wheel sure.  But i see two main issues. First is that it's easier to do an axial flux vs radial flux generator/motor on a bicycle wheel. Or mount a bunch of magnets on the outside rim and gonradial or axial flux. Secondly, while an athlete can do a bit better the average person may only generate roughly 100 watts of power continously on a bike for a length of time. But a bike wheel could be used as part of a wind turbine.

replies.author: @marcfruchtman9473
replies.text: @David_Mash Well, I had certainly considered it! But, the method they use is more like a "controlled" turning on / off of the coils using transistor technology. Technically it is "Brushless" but the transistor does the switching.
